common_name: Lang Jujube (Pear-Shaped Chinese Jujube, Large Late Jujube)

planting.note: Plant grafted trees in spring in full sun in any well-drained soil; Lang is one of the two most widely planted jujube varieties in the United States alongside Li — they are often planted together because cross-pollination between Lang and Li dramatically increases fruit set on both trees; Lang produces elongated, pear-shaped fruits that are larger than most jujubes and mature later in the season than Li.

directions: Plant in full sun in any well-drained soil — jujubes thrive in poor, alkaline, rocky soils where other fruit trees fail; Lang and Li are the classic companion pair for maximum fruit set; harvest fruits when they turn from green to mahogany-red and reach full size; air-dry excess harvest at room temperature for preserved 'dried dates.' Lang jujube is one of the two most important cultivated jujube varieties in American horticulture, planted alongside Li jujube (the other dominant variety) throughout the jujube-growing regions of the United States. Lang differs from Li in fruit shape and season: where Li produces round to oval fruits that ripen relatively early, Lang produces elongated, pear-shaped to teardrop-shaped fruits that are noticeably larger than Li (typically 1.5–2.5 inches long) and ripen later in the season. The larger, more elongated fruit shape of Lang also makes it particularly excellent for drying into Chinese-style red dates (hong zao / hóng zǎo), where the pear shape produces attractive elongated dried fruits with a pleasant chewy texture and concentrated honey-sweet flavor. Cross-pollination between Lang and Li is well-documented to significantly increase fruit set on both trees, making them the classic companion pair for backyard jujube plantings — planting one of each type provides mutual pollination benefits that result in substantially heavier crops than either variety planted alone. Like all jujubes, Lang is extremely drought-tolerant and heat-tolerant, thriving with no supplemental irrigation once established in most of the country. An exceptional choice for zone 9a and Gulf Coast gardeners who want a reliable, low-input fruit tree.
sources: Roger Meyer — California Rare Fruit Growers

common_name: Honey Jar Jujube (Small Sweet Early Chinese Jujube)

planting.note: Plant grafted tree in spring in full sun in any well-drained soil; Honey Jar is an early-maturing jujube variety producing small, very round fruits that ripen weeks ahead of Li and Lang — it is considered by many tasters to be the sweetest-flavored jujube available, with a flavor that lives up to its name of a fruit as sweet as concentrated honey.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil; Honey Jar is among the earliest jujubes to ripen (mid-August in many areas, 3–4 weeks before Li and Lang); the small fruits are best consumed fresh at peak ripeness rather than dried; trees are moderately compact compared to other jujube varieties. Honey Jar is widely regarded as the sweetest-tasting jujube variety available in American nurseries — the fruits are small (about 3/4 to 1 inch), round, and at perfect ripeness have a flavor described as pure concentrated honey with very little acidity and no bitterness, earning the variety its descriptive name. The small size, while meaning less fruit per piece, is actually an advantage in fresh eating situations where you want to pop a whole fruit in your mouth without cutting — Honey Jar is the jujube equivalent of a cherry tomato or Champagne grape, perfectly sized for snacking directly from the tree. The early ripening season (mid-August in zones 6–8, somewhat earlier in zone 9a) makes Honey Jar valuable for extending the fresh jujube harvest over the longest possible season when planted alongside the mid-season Li and the late-season Lang — a three-variety planting can provide fresh jujubes from late July through October. The trees are somewhat more compact than Li and Lang, making Honey Jar a good choice when space is limited. Cross-pollination with Lang or Li improves fruit set on Honey Jar trees significantly.
sources: Roger Meyer — Just Fruits and Exotics Nursery

common_name: Sherwood Jujube (Louisiana Jujube, Gulf Coast Large-Fruited Jujube)

planting.note: Plant grafted tree in spring in full sun in any well-drained soil; Sherwood is a Louisiana-originated jujube variety of particular importance for Gulf Coast gardeners — selected from a chance seedling that demonstrated superior adaptation to the hot, humid climate of coastal Louisiana, producing large, round fruits with excellent fresh-eating quality and robust disease resistance in conditions that challenge Chinese selections.

directions: Plant in full sun in any well-drained soil; Sherwood is vigorous and productive in the hot, humid Gulf Coast climate where some Chinese jujube selections may show reduced vigor; harvest at the red-mahogany stage for best fresh eating; the round, relatively large fruits are excellent for fresh eating and air-drying. Sherwood jujube is a Gulf Coast-adapted variety that originated as a chance seedling in Louisiana — making it an American selection rather than one of the Chinese-originated varieties (Li, Lang, Honey Jar) that dominate the market. This local origin is significant: the Sherwood tree's parents were jujubes that had already adapted to the climate of coastal Louisiana over multiple generations, and the resulting variety demonstrates the kind of humid-heat tolerance and disease resistance that matters in zone 9a and Gulf Coast conditions. Jujubes are generally very adaptable and drought-tolerant, but Chinese selections optimized for the dry summers of northern China sometimes show reduced performance in the hot, humid summers of the Gulf South. Sherwood's local Louisiana heritage makes it a logical first choice for Gulf Coast gardeners wanting to diversify beyond the standard Li and Lang combination. The fruits are large (comparable to Li), round, and flavorful at full mahogany-red maturity. Trees are vigorous and productive with good disease resistance in humid conditions. An important variety for preserving and developing Gulf Coast-adapted jujube germplasm.

common_name: Parfianka Pomegranate (Central Asian Premium Pomegranate, Soft-Seeded Supreme)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or grafted trees in spring in full sun in well-drained soil; Parfianka is widely considered by fruit connoisseurs to be the best-tasting pomegranate variety available in the United States, combining exceptional sweetness with complex flavor, very tender seeds (soft-seeded), and brilliant deep red arils — it consistently outranks all other varieties in blind tasting trials at California Rare Fruit Growers events.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil; pomegranates tolerate drought, alkaline soils, and heat — ideal for zone 9a; harvest when the skin deepens to full deep red and the fruit sounds hollow when tapped (late September–November in the Gulf South); Parfianka arils are strikingly deep red and the juice intensely colored. Parfianka is a Central Asian pomegranate variety (originally from Uzbekistan or the broader Turkestan region, where pomegranates have been cultivated for millennia) that has become the acknowledged gold standard of pomegranate flavor among American pomegranate enthusiasts, consistently winning blind tasting competitions at California Rare Fruit Growers Society events and receiving the highest ratings in UC Cooperative Extension pomegranate variety trials. The arils are brilliant deep crimson-red — darker and more richly colored than Wonderful (the commercial standard) — and have a flavor combination that separates Parfianka from other varieties: intensely sweet with balanced acidity, complex fruity aromatics, and remarkably soft, almost undetectable seeds. The seed softness (characteristic of the 'soft-seeded' varieties from Central Asia) means the arils can be eaten whole without the crunching sensation that bothers some pomegranate consumers. The juice is vivid deep red, exceptionally flavorful, and produces the finest pomegranate juice of any commercially available variety. For zone 9a and Gulf Coast gardeners willing to invest in the best possible fresh-eating pomegranate, Parfianka is the clear first choice — a tree that rewards with the finest-quality pomegranates available in home production.
sources: UC Cooperative Extension — Pomegranate Variety Trials

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun in well-drained soil; Eversweet is a unique pomegranate variety with clear, non-staining juice — unlike all other common pomegranates whose deep red juice stains hands and clothing permanently, Eversweet has very soft pink arils that produce a light pink juice that does not permanently stain — a practical advantage for families with children or for fresh eating over white fabric or clothing.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained to dry soil; Eversweet is extremely drought-tolerant and heat-tolerant once established, requiring little irrigation in most Gulf Coast settings after the first year; harvest when fruits are fully colored (rosy-orange to red skin) and sound hollow when tapped; fruits are sweet enough to be eaten with minimal mess. Eversweet is a distinctive pomegranate variety unlike any other in its combination of extreme sweetness, very soft (almost absent) seeds, and clear non-staining juice. Most pomegranates have deep red to crimson arils producing intensely colored red juice — the juice stains are notorious and pomegranate processing indoors is typically done over the kitchen sink with care to avoid contact with clothing. Eversweet breaks this rule: the arils are a soft rose-pink rather than deep red, and the juice they produce is clear to very light pink rather than the deep crimson of standard pomegranates, and crucially, it does not stain permanently. This characteristic makes Eversweet a more family-friendly choice for fresh eating, especially with children, and allows pomegranate eating and juicing without the usual mess concerns. The flavor is very sweet with low acidity — milder and less complex than Parfianka or Wonderful, but consistently rated among the sweetest pomegranates available. The seeds are extremely soft and virtually undetectable when eating the arils fresh. The exterior of the fruit is more orange-pink than red, making it visually distinct from other varieties at harvest.
sources: UC Cooperative Extension — Pomegranate Variety Trials
